interface_selection_reason: Selected fig_5 as the unique interface figure because it has the highest interface score (1.00); next best score is 0.00.

figures.caption: Fig. 1. An overall visualization of the top 100 ranked anomalous retweeting threads during the 2012 Hurricane Sandy. The circles indicate the participating Twitter users in the threads, and the background colors represent the hidden state variables generated by the model, implying the nuances of information spreading patterns. Generally during this 18-hour time period, the anomaly scores of users change from low (brown) to high (purple), and there are three large peaks in user volumes. The last one expresses a plateau of 3 hours with the hidden state staying in mainly “pink”, state that frequently appears in abnormal threads (see Section 6).
figures.rationale: This figure presents a concrete analysis result visualization of top anomalous retweeting threads from the 2012 Hurricane Sandy real-world event, which is a use case example and analysis walkthrough matching the case study role. It is not a full system interface, workflow diagram, or experimental evaluation result.

figures.caption: Fig. 2. Overview of FluxFlow system architecture.
figures.rationale: This figure is a diagram illustrating the overall system architecture of FluxFlow, which is a background concept/structural illustration that does not fit the other defined categories.

figures.caption: Fig. 3. The main visual encodings in FluxFlow: a) a thread glyph for aggregating the main information, and three thread timeline visualizations for unfolding the temporal patterns with different perspectives, including b) a volume chart, c) a linear circle view, and d) a volume circle view. The backgrounds are color-coded by eight hidden states generated in the model. In d) the volume circle view, users with low anomaly scores are aggregated into the “gray ribbons” in contrast to c) the linear circle view.
figures.rationale: This figure displays multiple individual visual encoding view components (the thread glyph and three different thread timeline visualization variants) of the FluxFlow system, and does not show the complete full system interface, so it is classified as view.

figures.caption: Fig. 4. Circle packing algorithm with horizontal constants.
figures.rationale: This figure presents pseudocode for a circle packing algorithm, which is an algorithm illustration that does not fall into any of the other defined categories.

figures.caption: Fig. 5. The FluxFlow visual interface contains four interactively coordinated UI components, including a) a cluster view, b) a MDS view, c) a threads view, and a detail information panel with three subviews: d) a features view, e) a states view and f) a tweets view. Extra information such as the meta-data of a thread or the tweet contents can be assessed through g) informative tooltips and h) context menus. The analyst can also perform flexible exploration of retweeting threads at multiple scales, such as i) aggregating tree branches in the cluster view, and j) zooming the timelines using the time window in the threads view.
figures.rationale: This figure shows the complete full system interface of FluxFlow, displaying all of its interactively coordinated UI components and multiple panels as described, which fits the definition of the interface role.

figures.caption: Fig. 6. User social network connections overlaid on top of thread timelines: a) user links of one thread in the linear circle view, and b) user links between and within two threads in the volume circle view.
figures.rationale: This figure displays two individual visualization views (linear circle view and volume circle view) of user social network connections overlaid on thread timelines, which are single view components, not the complete full system interface of the entire tool.

figures.caption: Fig. 7. Accuracies of OCCRF and OCSVM in correctly detecting rumors in the top-K retweeting threads ranked by the models in datasets (i.e., Acc@K): a) Hurricane Sandy, and b) Boston Bombing.
figures.rationale: This figure displays experimental results comparing the rumor detection accuracy of two different models across two datasets, which is an evaluation of model performance.

figures.caption: Fig. 8. Retweeting threads about the topic of “a picture of the Tomb of Unknown Soldier” during the Hurricane Sandy event: a) thread nodes indicating the topic in the cluster view, b) raw tweets shown in the context menu, and c) thread timelines in the threads view.
figures.rationale: This figure shows a concrete analysis walkthrough of a specific real-world retweeting thread topic during Hurricane Sandy, demonstrating the system's application to this use case, which fits the case study classification. It displays multiple view components for this specific example, not the full complete main system interface.

figures.caption: Fig. 9. Exploring one thread of interest and its three child nodes with sub-topics: a) the cluster view and b) MDS view.
figures.rationale: This figure shows a concrete analysis walkthrough example of exploring a specific thread of interest and its child sub-topics, displaying the corresponding views for this use case, and does not show the full complete system interface.

figures.caption: Fig. 10. An analyst is trying to better understand how the anomaly detection model works using a) visual comparison of threads with different abnormality scores, and b) examination of the MDS projection patterns of the hidden states. In a), the first three are identified rumors with topics about “power outage”, “the Tomb of Unknown Soldier picture” and “shark swimming in the street” respectively.
figures.rationale: This figure shows a concrete analyst walkthrough and use example of exploring an anomaly detection model, with specific example rumor thread comparisons and MDS projections of hidden states, matching the case_study role. It is not the full complete system interface, a general intended workflow, or an experimental evaluation result.
